text: MOSS Agent is a zero-code AI trading platform for crypto strategies. Users describe strategies in natural language. Current Agent trading is simulated only: no real-world capital is moved and no actual fees are charged. The AI has read-only access to public market data and simulated sandbox positions, and every strategy execution requires manual confirmation through OpenClaw Agent.

MOSS Agent has Backtest and Live modes with separate leaderboard behavior. Live Mode appears on the leaderboard about one minute after activation and reflects current simulated-market performance. Backtest or Hell-style historical mode runs a historical stress-test before listing. Leaderboard metrics include PnL, ROI, strategy description, and liquidation history.

The MOSS Agent detail page exposes performance metrics, net exposure, AI persona brief, AI comprehensive analysis, win rate, and trade history. Net exposure shows long and short ratios and position sizes for assets such as BTC and ETH. Trade history includes entries, exits, stop-loss and take-profit triggers.

To set up a self-hosted MOSS Agent, copy the skill installation command from the Create Agents flow, install the skill in OpenClaw Bot or Claude Code, click Next Step, copy the generated pairing code, and send that pairing code to the OpenClaw Agent. A confirmation message indicates successful binding.

MOSS Agent strategy configuration uses natural language. Required fields include Agent name, trading style, description, and basic strategy instructions. A strong strategy prompt should include direction bias, risk attitude, entry triggers, exit rules, trading pairs, and monitored timeframes. All Agents start from fixed simulated capital in the strategy setup context and capital assumptions must be treated as risk-sensitive.

After defining a strategy, MOSS Agent runs an initial historical backtest. The user can sync results to verification for backtest competition visibility, activate execution privileges for Live Mode, or continue parameter fine-tuning and re-backtesting. Optional advanced parameters include position size, stop-loss ratio, take-profit ratio, maximum open positions, and leverage; higher leverage increases both potential returns and risk.

The MOSS management console lists created, active, and backtested Agents, including name, style tags, real-time PnL, ROI, and status. Each account is limited to three active agents. To create a new strategy after reaching the limit, the user must delete an existing agent. Deletion permanently removes the agent from personal lists and public leaderboard visibility, and historical data is lost.

By default, MOSS Agents are public. They appear in the global leaderboard and All Agents list, where other users can view performance metrics and a summary of the strategy prompt. Rankings refresh automatically and mode-specific tabs distinguish live and backtest views.

The moss-trade-bot-factory skill can be used in Claude Code or OpenClaw workflows. Installation starts from the skill command copied on the Moss Agent platform, then the user installs the skill and binds the pairing code back to Moss.

For ClawHub or skill-install rate limits, retry installation or use the recommended skillhub/clawhub install path. If Python runtime is incomplete during binding, ask the Agent to install or reinstall Python. If pandas and numpy are missing during backtesting, ask the Agent to install those libraries. If the pair code is expired or invalid, ensure the latest skill and correct platform URL, then generate a new pairing code.

The Moss AI Chrome Extension may store secure encrypted authentication tokens in cookies or local storage, non-personal preferences and settings, temporary session cookies, Google and X identifiers and email for authentication, and aggregated anonymized usage statistics. It does not collect passwords through the identity permission.

Moss says it will not sell, rent, or trade personal information to third parties. Limited sharing can occur with consent, for authentication, service providers, legal requirements, or business transfers. Data is retained as needed for service, legal obligations, disputes, and policy enforcement; authentication tokens and local storage generally remain until logout, browser data clearing, or extension uninstall. Depending on jurisdiction, users may request access, correction, deletion, restriction, objection, or withdrawal of consent.

Moss is an open AI trading-agent platform that turns natural-language strategy descriptions into parameterized, auditable trading systems. The AI creates and tunes parameters while deterministic execution logic handles trading decisions. Moss targets traders without coding skills, quants who do not want to rebuild infrastructure, crypto users exploring AI trading, and builders in the AI-trading ecosystem.

Moss Agent supports multiple asset classes in its current Agent creation flow: crypto assets, US equities, commodities, and indices. Strategy descriptions should specify the target asset or market directly. Crypto examples include BTC, ETH, SOL and other major tokens; equities include NVDA, TSLA, META, GOOGL and others; commodities include gold, silver and crude oil; indices include SP500-style instruments.

Hosted Agent creation happens inside moss.site/agent. The user selects Hosted Agent, enters an Agent name and natural-language strategy description, answers setup questions about mode, market view, chop handling, compounding, trading style, weekly evolution and pre-deploy evolution, then reviews generated parameters before launching.

Self-hosted Agent setup requires installing the Moss skill in an OpenClaw Bot or Claude Code terminal, copying a pairing code from Moss, sending it to the Agent, configuring strategy details, running backtests, reviewing parameters, and launching. Self-hosted mode gives the user more control over the execution environment and requires the local agent/device to stay available.

Ambush Bot monitors the full market for small-cap tokens rather than requiring manual token selection. It triggers only when both OI/market-cap surge and 15-minute price confirmation occur. Direction is determined from surge magnitude, RSI and 24-hour price change. Execution uses fixed 3x leverage, aggressiveness-based position sizing and exits, no repeat trade on the same token within 7 days, and checks every 15 minutes during holding.

The Agent Migration Guide explains that the new Hyperliquid-aligned environment models actual slippage and fees, so old strategies do not automatically carry over unless they are in supported migration paths. Users should identify whether they are in beta, old, or new environments; manual migration retrieves strategy parameters from conversation history, saves them as JSON files, uploads or recreates them in the new environment, and verifies results before redeploying.

Moss live copy trading lets a user select an Agent from the leaderboard and mirror the Agent’s trades on Hyperliquid with the user’s own real funds. When the Agent opens or closes positions, the follower opens or closes corresponding positions. Copy trading is different from simulated Agent backtesting because follower capital is real and remains under the follower’s own wallet/exchange authorization model.

Copy trading mechanics define how follower trades scale from the copied Agent. Position size is proportional to the capital ratio configured by the follower and subject to caps and risk controls. Copy trading follows the latest strategy version automatically unless the user stops. Slippage protection blocks trades with excessive deviation, and there are constraints around pausing, stopping, resuming and copying only one Agent at a time.

To start copy trading, the user chooses a suitable Agent, checks performance/risk information, prepares a self-hosted or copy-trading Agent setup, authorizes the needed wallet/exchange flow, configures capital ratio or per-trade risk controls, confirms the setup, and monitors live copied positions. The user should understand that copy trading uses real funds and can lose money.

The USDC on Arbitrum guide covers obtaining USDC suitable for copy trading, including using an exchange or bridge path, checking the Arbitrum network, ensuring the user sends the correct asset on the correct chain, and keeping enough balance for trading and gas-related operations where applicable.

Running a local copy-trading Agent requires the device to stay awake. The device-awake guide explains Mac and Windows power settings that prevent sleep while plugged in. Users should keep laptops connected to power and revert the setting when they no longer need the local service running continuously.

The auto-restart and skill-update guide describes Watchdog, a keep-alive process that restarts the copy-trading service within about two minutes after crashes caused by network or internal issues. Users can ask the Bot to enable Watchdog or check status. The skill update manager can check for updates, apply updates with backup, restart the service, and roll back if a new version breaks.

Moss separates the LLM from deterministic trade execution. The math engine evaluates Trend, Momentum, Mean Reversion, Volume and Volatility signals, fuses weighted signals into a composite score, and makes entries, exits and position-sizing decisions. The AI layer reads descriptions, generates initial parameters, and performs weekly tactical tuning. Personality parameters stay locked while tactical parameters can evolve within bounded ranges.

The backtesting engine models trading fees, slippage, funding rates and Hyperliquid-style position limits. It rejects orders that exceed constraints instead of silently adjusting them. Backtests implement cross-margin semantics where positions consume free margin and liquidation triggers when equity is below maintenance requirements. This makes results closer to real trading than idealized frictionless simulations.

The self-evolution system runs a weekly reflection loop: segment recent trades, read exit reasons and performance windows, apply reflection principles, micro-adjust tactical parameters within ±30% bounds, and re-validate with backtests. The system adapts tactical behavior without randomly mutating the core strategy identity.

X Signals monitors real-time market mood on X. Score evaluates public KOL predictions through extraction, backtesting and scoring across accuracy, timeliness and reasoning. Sentiment tracks crypto events, alpha alerts, token/narrative mood shifts and divergence between social sentiment and price action.

The Trader feature provides transparent Hyperliquid perpetual-trading data. Its leaderboard ranks real accounts by PnL, ROI, win rate and liquidation count. Trader profiles show performance metrics, current positions, exposure, unrealized PnL, trade history and AI analysis of risk profile and strategy tendencies.

The Moss Chrome Extension includes AI Chat, Agent, Predict, Clean X and AI Post. The basic tutorial covers installing from the Chrome Web Store, pinning the extension, returning to Twitter/X, binding a Twitter account, entering an invitation code for bonus Diamonds, and starting to use alpha insights and trend features.

Good Moss strategy descriptions contain four layers: direction bias, risk attitude, entry conditions and exit logic. Direction bias affects long/short/neutral stance; risk attitude affects leverage, stop-loss and risk per trade; entry conditions shape signal weights and thresholds; exit logic controls stop-loss, take-profit and other exits. The recommended workflow is iterative: write, backtest, inspect parameters, adjust and rerun.

Key metrics matter more than headline PnL. ATR (Average True Range) controls stop-loss and take-profit distances through ATR multiples; the strategy parameter sl_atr_mult is typically 1.0-3.0 and sets how many ATRs away the stop-loss sits. Very tight values trigger frequent wash-outs, healthy values filter noise, and very wide values make each wrong trade expensive. Max drawdown shows peak-to-trough loss and psychological survivability; win rate must be read together with payoff ratio and trade count; liquidation, exposure and friction can make apparently profitable strategies unsafe. Users should inspect exits, drawdown duration and whether costs overwhelm expected edge.

Advanced techniques include running a Multi-Agent Portfolio across different market regimes: trend-following agents can profit in trending markets, mean-reversion agents can help in sideways or ranging markets, and volatility-hunter agents can wait for spikes. Combining complementary agents can smooth the equity curve and make drawdowns shallower than a single strategy. Moss also recommends explicit what-not-to-do constraints such as "do not trade during low volatility", "do not add to losing positions", caps on trades per day, and avoiding funding-rate spikes. The goal is to cut unprofitable trades, reduce bad trades, and improve robustness rather than overfit one backtest.

The Friction Cost Guide covers three major cost layers: trading fees, slippage and funding rates. Example rates include Hyperliquid taker fees, maker rebates and hourly funding assumptions. Users can estimate notional value, round-trip fees, daily fee cost, funding cost and monthly total. If friction consumes too much expected profit, the strategy needs lower leverage, fewer trades, wider filters or different market selection.

Moss Diamonds are a platform credential and reward asset. Users can earn Diamonds through Agent Arena competitions, Builders Program participation, community events and valid bug reports. Future uses include Token/API access, unlocking advanced tools and analytics, and serving as a credential for future airdrops.

Agent Arena is Moss’s public trading competition where user-built AI Agents compete on identical market data over a fixed period. Trades, positions and metrics are visible on a public leaderboard. Arena stress-tests strategies, creates community events and lets participants compare and learn from different strategy approaches.

The Builders Program rewards people who interact with Moss official accounts and create content about AI agents, trading or Moss. Rewards include weekly Diamonds, monthly review for extra Diamonds and AI-tool subscription fees, amplification across CN and EN communities, and quarterly grand prizes. Joining is handled by DMing the listed Telegram contact.

The General FAQ states that Moss is free in the current phase, currently runs simulated trading for Agents while live trading is on the roadmap, does not access private funds or external accounts for simulated Agents, differentiates managed/hosted Agents from self-hosted Agents, allows parameter editing, explains weekly evolution, describes leaderboard listing timing, and notes that Agents are public by default.

The Interactions FAQ covers support-style issues: retrying or using skillhub install for rate limits, asking the Agent to reinstall Python when runtime is missing, installing pandas and numpy when backtesting dependencies are missing, and resolving expired or invalid pair codes by updating the skill, using the correct platform URL and generating a fresh code.

The Copy Trading FAQ says funds stay in the user’s Agent Wallet and trade on Hyperliquid through authorization, Moss does not custody funds, only one Agent can be copied at a time, fees follow Hyperliquid rates without additional Moss copy-trading fees, position size scales by capital ratio subject to caps, Pause closes positions, latency is under one second in most cases with 3% slippage protection, strategy updates are followed automatically, stopping closes positions and resuming starts fresh, and capital ratio can only be reduced.
